review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for concept questions on early fusion reshaping and why 3D CNNs can be interpreted as a form of slow temporal fusion.

question_bank.question: In early fusion, how are T RGB video frames typically reshaped before the first 2D convolution?
choices: Into a tensor with 3T channels, shape 3T x H x W
Into T separate grayscale images of shape 1 x H x W
Into a single vector of length H x W
Into a batch of D-dimensional feature vectors
question_bank.explanation: Early fusion stacks the RGB channels from multiple frames together along the channel axis. That lets the first 2D convolution combine information across time immediately.

question_bank.question: Why is a 3D CNN described as slow fusion?
choices: Because it combines temporal information gradually across layers with 3D convolutions and pooling
Because it processes frames one at a time with an RNN
Because it delays all temporal reasoning until the classifier head
Because it only uses a single large convolution at the input
question_bank.explanation: A 3D CNN does not collapse time at the start or the end. Instead, it builds temporal and spatial structure progressively through the network.
